lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market firms operating gyms, fitness centers, fitness studios, swimming facilities, skating rinks, and racquet or other recreational sports centers for external members and repeat users.
lens.excluded_activities: Government or captive employee facilities, passive property or financing vehicles, non-control interests, independent instructors without an operated facility, and sports instruction businesses classified outside NAICS 713940.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Consumers and organizations pay recurring monthly or annual memberships plus enrollment, class, training, court, pool, program, day-pass, event, and ancillary retail fees; some facilities operate through franchises or nonprofit ownership.

a.rationale: The exact six-digit occupation mix bounds the opportunity. In May 2023, exercise trainers and group fitness instructors were 30.94% of employment, office and administrative support 17.00%, amusement and recreation attendants 8.13%, management 5.55%, sales 4.19%, lifeguards and related protective workers 4.51%, and janitors 2.97%. AI can expose much of lead follow-up, billing support, scheduling, routine member communication, reporting, marketing content, and workout-plan drafting, plus portions of front-desk and manager paperwork. It exposes much less of live instruction, coaching, cleaning, maintenance, lifeguarding, equipment supervision, hospitality, and in-person sales. Applying task shares to occupation wages yields this bounded judgment rather than an observed automation share.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted time-and-motion study from 20-30 clubs and studios, separating already automated work from current employee hours across front desk, sales, billing, member service, management, trainers, instructors, cleaning, maintenance, and aquatics, would materially change the estimate.
caveats: OEWS measures occupations and wages, not task-level AI exposure or current software adoption.
The NAICS mix spans low-staff gyms, instructor-led studios, pools, skating rinks, and racquet centers with very different labor models.
Contract instructors and owner labor may be incompletely represented in employer payroll data.

rho.rationale: Commercial club platforms already integrate membership records, payments, scheduling, reporting, automated follow-up, AI-assisted communication, and billing recovery, making the administrative opportunity technically implementable. Five-year realization is reduced for fragmented member data, legacy access-control and payment systems, franchise constraints, privacy and payment compliance, model errors in member communication or exercise guidance, and the change burden across part-time workforces.
rho.change_evidence: Matched club-level implementations reporting staff hours per member, billing recovery, lead conversion, complaint rates, retention, override frequency, and sustained user adoption for at least 24 months would materially tighten the range.
caveats: Software feature availability does not establish adoption or realized labor avoidance.
Large franchised systems have more standardized data and rollout capacity than independent LMM operators.
Implementation excludes pricing, demand, and valuation effects.

e.rationale: The NAICS definition centers on operated physical facilities offering exercise and recreational sports to users, and the common membership, class, and access model is recurring or repeat external service. Deductions allow for nonprofit or quasi-public operators, captive facilities, entities whose economics are primarily property ownership, distressed shells, and franchise or lease situations where control is not practically transferable.
e.change_evidence: A census of the supplied 937 estimated LMM firms coded for ownership form, external revenue share, franchise transfer rights, lease assignability, EBITDA quality, and control availability would directly measure eligibility.
caveats: No source measures eligibility within the supplied LMM population.
The code includes materially different facility types and nonprofit ownership is more common in some subsegments.
The supplied n is a margin-bridged estimate rather than an observed count of normalized-EBITDA firms.

s5.rationale: A functioning sale market is evidenced by five years of reported gym and fitness-center transactions on BizBuySell, and branded systems explicitly permit franchise transfers and report refranchising activity. Broad US succession pressure is meaningful because 51% of small-business owners are reported to be over age 55. The estimate remains below a simple retirement share because many older owners do not exit within five years and some outcomes are closures, family or internal reorganizations, minority investments, or transfers blocked by leases and franchisors.
s5.change_evidence: A deduplicated 2021-2026 dataset from BizBuySell, DealStats, Axial, franchise disclosure documents, and major franchisors matching completed control transfers to eligible firms and distinguishing closures, asset sales, and internal reorganizations would materially change the estimate.
caveats: BizBuySell publishes transaction benchmarks but not a complete LMM firm-level transfer denominator on the fetched page.
Planet Fitness transfers are for one branded multi-unit system and are not representative of all facility types.
The owner-age statistic covers US small businesses generally rather than fitness-center owners.

q.rationale: Monthly and annual membership dues are fixed recurring prices rather than cost-plus reimbursement, so administrative savings are not contractually passed through. Planet Fitness reported more than 90% of club and franchise revenue from recurring streams and described predictable centralized billing, while also identifying rent and labor as fixed or semi-fixed. Retention is reduced by transparent local pricing, aggressive promotional offers, membership churn, franchise royalties and required marketing, and the need to reinvest in staff, equipment, cleanliness, and member experience.
q.change_evidence: Five-year club cohorts linking automation-related labor savings to membership prices, discounts, cancellation rates, acquisition cost, service levels, royalty burden, and four-wall margins would directly measure benefit retention.
caveats: Planet Fitness is a scaled, value-priced franchise and may have different pricing power and operating leverage from independent clubs and boutique studios.
The estimate discounts future sharing through renewal pricing and competition but excludes demand volume effects.
Some savings may be absorbed by higher wages, equipment refresh, rent, insurance, or increased service rather than retained as profit.

d5.bridge: HFA measures members and visits across US gyms, studios, and other fitness facilities rather than constant-price output of eligible LMM firms. Membership reached 81 million in 2025, up 5.2% from 2024, with nearly 7 billion visits; first-half 2025 visits were up 3.5% year over year across a tracker covering nearly 11,000 facilities. The five-year range assumes recent gains normalize to roughly flat through moderate annual quantity growth rather than compounding 5.2% unchanged. It may err upward if new capacity, low-price chains, or home fitness displace LMM incumbents, and downward if health, social, and active-aging participation continues broadening.
d5.rationale: Facility membership, visitation, and engagement are at record levels, with growth across age and income groups and especially strong older-adult growth. This supports expanding aggregate demand, tempered by discretionary spending exposure, local overbuilding, format shifts, member churn, and substitution among budget clubs, premium clubs, studios, outdoor activity, and home fitness.
d5.change_evidence: A five-year panel of inflation-adjusted dues, active memberships, visits, paid sessions, usable capacity, openings, closures, and member churn by facility format and independent-versus-chain ownership would materially improve the estimate.
caveats: HFA membership and visitation include facilities outside the LMM firm lens and do not adjust for quality or market-share shifts.
Recent growth follows pandemic disruption and may not represent a steady five-year rate.
Planet Fitness growth reflects one scaled format and includes international clubs in some reported totals.

o.rationale: The current basket requires accountable operation of physical space, equipment, access, cleaning, maintenance, safety, pools or courts where present, and a member experience. Digital sign-up, access control, virtual programming, AI workout plans, and self-service support can reduce staffed interactions, but record facility visits indicate that software is mostly complementary to the venue rather than a full substitute. Some instructor-led demand can migrate to apps or video, and low-staff club formats can automate more of the service interface.
o.change_evidence: Operating comparisons of staffed and unstaffed or hybrid facilities covering visits, incidents, equipment downtime, cleaning quality, cancellations, instructor hours, regulatory staffing, and customer-support escalation would materially move the range.
caveats: Operator-required share differs sharply between access-only gyms and pools, skating rinks, racquet clubs, and instructor-led studios.
The estimate concerns accountable operators, not the number of on-site employees.
Changes in liability rules, remote monitoring, and access-control technology could alter the result.

narrative.executive_view: Fitness and recreational sports centers have strong recurring billing, broad consumer participation, and a meaningful administrative labor layer, but their core service remains a physical venue with trainers, attendants, cleaning, maintenance, and safety obligations. AI can improve front-office economics and member engagement without replacing most service delivery. The opportunity is vulnerable to local competition, member churn, lease and equipment commitments, franchise restrictions, and the wide variation between gyms, studios, pools, rinks, and racquet facilities.
narrative.ai_impact: The clearest uses are lead response, membership sales support, scheduling, billing recovery, routine member service, marketing creation, reporting, staff administration, and workout-plan drafting. Live coaching, group instruction, lifeguarding, cleaning, maintenance, equipment supervision, and high-empathy retention conversations remain substantially human and on-site.
narrative.value_capture: Fixed monthly and annual dues let operators retain savings more readily than cost-plus businesses, and automated billing makes revenue predictable. Competitive promotions and low switching costs constrain retention, while franchisor fees, local marketing, service reinvestment, and member expectations absorb part of the gross benefit.
narrative.firm_availability: Most LMM operators should qualify as repeat external services, and both marketplace transactions and franchise transfers show that clubs can change control. The direct five-year transfer rate is not observed, however, and leases, franchisor approval, nonprofit ownership, owner dependence, and distressed closures reduce practical transferability.
narrative.demand_durability: US facility memberships and visits reached records in 2025, with broad demographic participation and strong active-aging growth. Aggregate demand appears durable, but individual firms can lose share quickly to new budget chains, premium formats, studios, home fitness, or nearby facilities, especially when capital expenditure and cleanliness lag.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: The key gaps are establishment-level task adoption, realized AI labor savings, firm-level eligibility, and a denominator-matched LMM transfer series. Occupation data predate the current AI cycle, trade-association growth may not accrue to independents, and scaled public franchises may not represent smaller facilities. Lease liabilities, equipment replacement, safety incidents, seasonality, and local saturation can overwhelm administrative savings.
narrative.principal_driver: Recurring membership billing and a sizable front-office workforce create a practical path to retain AI-enabled administrative savings.
narrative.principal_weakness: Most value still depends on a competitive, capital-intensive physical facility and in-person service that software cannot eliminate.

sources.title: 2022 NAICS Definition: 713940 Fitness and Recreational Sports Centers
sources.supports: Defines the industry as establishments operating fitness and recreational sports facilities for exercise, conditioning, swimming, skating, and racquet sports; supports the facility-based lens and heterogeneous service basket.

sources.title: Fitness and Recreational Sports Centers - May 2023 OEWS Industry-Specific Occupational Employment and Wage Estimates
sources.supports: Reports 594,740 total workers and exact six-digit occupation shares, including exercise trainers and group fitness instructors at 30.94%, office and administrative support at 17.00%, amusement and recreation attendants at 8.13%, management at 5.55%, sales at 4.19%, lifeguards and related workers at 4.51%, and janitors at 2.97%; anchors the wage-weighted task assessment.

sources.title: Daxko Club Automation
sources.supports: Documents currently available club software for membership management, payments, scheduling, reporting, automated follow-up, AI-powered communication, and billing recovery; supports exposure and implementation feasibility, not realized savings.

sources.title: 81 Million Americans Were Members of a Fitness Facility in 2025, New HFA Report Finds
sources.supports: Reports 81 million US fitness-facility members in 2025, up 5.2%, more than 100 million total users, nearly 7 billion visits, 26.1% population penetration, and broad demographic growth; supports demand durability and continued use of physical facilities.

sources.title: 2025 Fitness Industry Foot Traffic Trends: Budget Gyms Booming, Engagement Up Across All Segments
sources.supports: Reports first-half 2025 US fitness-facility visits up 3.5% year over year and monthly visits per user up 1.4%, based on a tracker covering nearly 11,000 facilities; supports the demand proxy and format competition.

sources.title: Planet Fitness, Inc. 2025 Form 10-K
sources.supports: Reports recurring monthly and annual billing, more than 90% recurring club and franchise revenue, predictable centralized billing, fixed or semi-fixed rent and labor, 20.8 million members, 2,896 clubs, franchise transfer fees, and 8 clubs refranchised in 2025; supports revenue structure, capture, operator need, and transferability.

sources.title: Gym & Fitness Center Business Valuation Multiples & Financial Benchmarks
sources.supports: Provides transaction-derived benchmarks for gym and fitness-center businesses sold from 2021 through 2025, confirming an operating-business sale market and describing the size profile of reported sales; it does not provide a complete industry transfer denominator.

sources.title: Starting a small business is hard. Exiting can be even harder, but planning early is the key
sources.supports: Reports that 51% of US small-business owners are over age 55 and describes outsider sales, family succession, internal succession, and closure as competing exit paths; used as broad succession context.
